title: An Open Letter to the People Who Pass Us at Six
summary: An open letter, addressed by a night-shift office cleaner in Toronto to the daytime workers she never meets, that resists both pity and the tidy slogan that 'all work is dignified', insisting instead on a stranger and harder kind of recognition.
body:
To the people who arrive as I am leaving:

You have never seen me, though we share a building and, in a sense, a life. I clean the thirty-first floor between two and five in the morning, and by the time your lifts begin to fill at eight, I am asleep in a flat you would not be able to find. We overlap for perhaps four minutes a day, at the revolving door, where you are arriving caffeinated and I am leaving emptied, and in those four minutes you are unfailingly polite and entirely incurious. I am not writing to scold you for that. Incuriosity is not cruelty. I am writing because I have been thinking about a particular kind of kindness that I would rather you did not offer me.

Now and then one of you stops. You tell me, with real warmth, that what I do is important, that the building could not function without me, that cleaning is dignified work and you have nothing but respect for it. I want to be careful here, because you mean well and meaning well is not nothing. But I have come to distrust that sentence. It is a sentence people say to feel that they have noticed something, and the feeling of having noticed is, for them, the end of the matter. You say the work is dignified and then you go up to the thirty-first floor I have just made habitable for you, and the dignity, having been declared, requires nothing further of either of us.

Here is the thing I would ask you to sit with, uncomfortably. The dignity of my work is not the point, and insisting on it can be a way of avoiding the point. A great deal of what I do is genuinely tedious, and some of it is unpleasant, and on the bad nights it is lonely in a way that no amount of respect from a passing stranger can touch. To tell me it is dignified is to ask me to supply you with a comforting story about myself, so that you do not have to hold the less comfortable fact that a person can do necessary work, do it well, and still find it hard, and still deserve more than a kind sentence at a revolving door.

What I would value is smaller and stranger than respect. I would value being known to exist in particular. Not 'cleaners' as a noble abstraction, but the fact that the woman who empties your bin has a name, which is on a card you have never read, and a reason she works at night, which is that the night rate is higher and she has a daughter at the University of Waterloo whose fees do not care how dignified anyone finds the labour that pays them. I am not asking you to learn this. I am pointing out that you could, and that you have decided, without deciding, that you will not.

Do not mistake this for bitterness. I am, on balance, alright. The work has given me a strange expertise in the off-hours architecture of the city — the rhythm of the cleaners and guards and bakers and nurses who keep the place breathing while it sleeps, a whole society that hands the building to yours at dawn like a relay baton you never notice you are taking. We are not invisible to each other. We know each other's names down here. It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop.

So I will leave you with the one request that survives all this. The next time someone tells you a kind of work is dignified, ask yourself whether you are honouring the worker or excusing yourself from the harder labour of seeing one. The two feel almost identical from the inside. That, I have come to think, is exactly why the kind sentence is so popular, and so cheap.

Yours, from the floor you will walk on in three hours,
the woman you will not see.

annotations.meaningVi: đến đúng lúc tôi rời đi (hai thế giới chỉ chạm nhau thoáng qua)

annotations.phrase: share a building and, in a sense, a life
annotations.meaningVi: cùng chung một tòa nhà và, theo một nghĩa nào đó, một cuộc đời
đã nạp cà phê, tỉnh táo nhờ cafein
đã cạn kiệt, rỗng tuếch (về thể chất và tinh thần)
lịch sự không bao giờ sai sót
hoàn toàn không tò mò, không quan tâm tìm hiểu
mắng mỏ, quở trách
Sự thờ ơ không phải là độc ác — tác giả phân biệt rạch ròi
với sự ấm áp thật lòng
công việc đáng tôn trọng, có phẩm giá
có ý tốt không phải là vô nghĩa (vẫn đáng kể)
dần dần hoài nghi câu nói đó
chấm dứt vấn đề (không cần làm gì thêm)
làm cho có thể ở/làm việc được (sạch sẽ)

annotations.phrase: having been declared, requires nothing further
annotations.meaningVi: một khi đã được tuyên bố thì không đòi hỏi gì thêm — phẩm giá thành lời nói suông
ngồi lại đối diện với (điều khó chịu) thay vì né tránh
tẻ nhạt, nhàm chán đến phát mệt

annotations.phrase: no amount of respect from a passing stranger can touch
annotations.meaningVi: không lượng tôn trọng nào từ một người lạ thoáng qua có thể chạm tới/xoa dịu được

annotations.phrase: supply you with a comforting story
annotations.meaningVi: cung cấp cho bạn một câu chuyện an ủi (để bạn yên lòng)
một khái niệm trừu tượng cao quý (chứ không phải con người cụ thể)
đổ thùng rác của bạn
mức lương ca đêm cao hơn
mà học phí của con bé chẳng quan tâm (nhân hóa: học phí vô cảm)
đã quyết định mà không hề ý thức là mình quyết định

annotations.phrase: Do not mistake this for bitterness
annotations.meaningVi: Đừng nhầm điều này với sự cay đắng
xét cho cùng, cân nhắc mọi mặt
kiến trúc của thành phố vào những giờ vắng — ẩn dụ cho thế giới về đêm

annotations.phrase: keep the place breathing while it sleeps
annotations.meaningVi: giữ cho nơi này thở trong khi nó ngủ — những người làm đêm duy trì sự sống của thành phố
như một cây gậy tiếp sức (trong chạy tiếp sức)

annotations.phrase: It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop
annotations.meaningVi: Chỉ khi hướng lên trên, về phía bạn, thì những cái tên mới ngừng lại — bất bình đẳng của sự được biết đến

annotations.phrase: excusing yourself from the harder labour of seeing one
annotations.meaningVi: tự miễn cho mình khỏi lao động khó hơn là thực sự nhìn thấy một người
phổ biến đến vậy, và rẻ tiền đến vậy — câu khen ngợi không tốn gì

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences with 'It is ... that' for contrastive focus
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ 'It is X that...' tách một thành phần ra để nhấn mạnh sự tương phản. Trong bài: 'It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop.' Cấu trúc này dồn trọng tâm vào 'upward, toward you', làm bật bất bình đẳng: ở tầng dưới ai cũng có tên, chỉ hướng lên trên mới mất.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: An 'it'-cleft isolates one element for contrastive stress: 'It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop.' The cleft forces the reader to weigh the focused phrase ('only upward, toward you') against an unstated alternative (downward, among the night workers, names persist). Compare the flat version — 'The names stop only upward' — which loses the pointed contrast.

translationVi:
Gửi những người đến đúng lúc tôi rời đi:

Bạn chưa bao giờ thấy tôi, dù chúng ta chung một tòa nhà và, theo một nghĩa nào đó, một cuộc đời. Tôi lau tầng ba mươi mốt từ hai đến năm giờ sáng, và đến khi thang máy của bạn bắt đầu đầy người lúc tám giờ, tôi đã ngủ trong một căn hộ mà bạn sẽ không thể tìm ra. Chúng ta giao nhau có lẽ bốn phút mỗi ngày, ở cửa xoay, nơi bạn đang đến với đầy cafein còn tôi đang rời đi trong trạng thái cạn kiệt, và trong bốn phút đó bạn luôn lịch sự không sai sót và hoàn toàn không tò mò. Tôi không viết để quở trách bạn vì điều đó. Sự thờ ơ không phải là độc ác. Tôi viết vì tôi đã suy nghĩ về một loại lòng tốt cụ thể mà tôi mong bạn đừng dành cho tôi.

Thỉnh thoảng một người trong số các bạn dừng lại. Bạn nói với tôi, bằng sự ấm áp thật lòng, rằng việc tôi làm là quan trọng, rằng tòa nhà không thể vận hành thiếu tôi, rằng lau dọn là công việc có phẩm giá và bạn chẳng có gì ngoài sự tôn trọng dành cho nó. Tôi muốn thận trọng ở đây, vì bạn có ý tốt và có ý tốt không phải là vô nghĩa. Nhưng tôi đã dần hoài nghi câu nói đó. Đó là câu người ta nói để cảm thấy mình đã chú ý đến điều gì đó, và cái cảm giác đã chú ý ấy, với họ, là sự chấm dứt vấn đề. Bạn nói công việc có phẩm giá rồi bạn lên tầng ba mươi mốt mà tôi vừa làm cho ở được vì bạn, và phẩm giá, một khi đã được tuyên bố, không đòi hỏi gì thêm ở cả hai chúng ta.

Đây là điều tôi muốn bạn ngồi lại đối diện, một cách khó chịu. Phẩm giá của công việc tôi không phải là điểm mấu chốt, và khăng khăng về nó có thể là một cách né tránh điểm mấu chốt. Phần lớn việc tôi làm thật sự tẻ nhạt, và một số thì khó chịu, và vào những đêm tồi tệ nó cô đơn theo cách mà không lượng tôn trọng nào từ một người lạ thoáng qua có thể chạm tới. Bảo tôi rằng nó có phẩm giá là yêu cầu tôi cung cấp cho bạn một câu chuyện an ủi về chính tôi, để bạn không phải ôm lấy sự thật kém dễ chịu hơn rằng một người có thể làm công việc cần thiết, làm tốt, mà vẫn thấy nó khó khăn, và vẫn xứng đáng được nhiều hơn một câu nói tử tế ở cửa xoay.

Điều tôi sẽ trân trọng nhỏ hơn và lạ hơn sự tôn trọng. Tôi sẽ trân trọng việc được biết đến như một cá thể cụ thể. Không phải 'những người lau dọn' như một khái niệm cao quý trừu tượng, mà sự thật rằng người phụ nữ đổ thùng rác của bạn có một cái tên, in trên tấm thẻ bạn chưa bao giờ đọc, và một lý do để làm ca đêm, đó là mức lương đêm cao hơn và cô có một đứa con gái học tại Đại học Waterloo mà học phí của con bé chẳng quan tâm ai thấy công việc trả tiền cho nó có phẩm giá đến đâu. Tôi không yêu cầu bạn học điều này. Tôi đang chỉ ra rằng bạn có thể, và rằng bạn đã quyết định, mà không hề ý thức là mình quyết định, rằng bạn sẽ không.

Đừng nhầm điều này với sự cay đắng. Xét cho cùng, tôi vẫn ổn. Công việc đã cho tôi một sự thông thạo kỳ lạ về kiến trúc giờ vắng của thành phố — nhịp điệu của những người lau dọn, bảo vệ, thợ làm bánh và y tá giữ cho nơi này thở trong khi nó ngủ, cả một xã hội trao tòa nhà cho xã hội của bạn lúc bình minh như một cây gậy tiếp sức mà bạn không bao giờ để ý là mình đang nhận. Chúng tôi không vô hình với nhau. Chúng tôi biết tên nhau ở dưới này. Chỉ khi hướng lên trên, về phía bạn, thì những cái tên mới ngừng lại.

Vậy nên tôi sẽ để lại cho bạn một lời thỉnh cầu duy nhất sống sót sau tất cả những điều này. Lần tới khi ai đó nói với bạn rằng một loại công việc nào đó có phẩm giá, hãy tự hỏi liệu bạn đang tôn vinh người lao động hay đang tự miễn cho mình khỏi lao động khó hơn là thực sự nhìn thấy một người. Hai điều đó cảm giác gần như giống hệt nhau từ bên trong. Đó, tôi đã đi đến chỗ nghĩ vậy, chính là lý do câu nói tử tế ấy phổ biến đến thế, và rẻ tiền đến thế.

Thân ái, từ sàn nhà bạn sẽ bước lên trong ba tiếng nữa,
người phụ nữ bạn sẽ không nhìn thấy.

questions.prompt: Who is the letter addressed to?
options: The daytime office workers who arrive as the writer is leaving
questions.explanation: 'To the people who arrive as I am leaving' — the daytime workers she overlaps with for four minutes.
questions.hint: The salutation and first paragraph identify the audience.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say she is NOT writing to scold the reader for being incurious?
options: Because she is too tired to be angry
Because the readers tip her generously
Because she fears losing her job
questions.explanation: 'I am not writing to scold you for that. Incuriosity is not cruelty.'

questions.prompt: What kind of kindness does the writer say she would rather NOT be offered?
options: Money or tips at the door
Being told that her cleaning work is dignified
An invitation to join the daytime staff
questions.explanation: 'You tell me ... that cleaning is dignified work ... But I have come to distrust that sentence.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 2 names the specific compliment she distrusts.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer distrust the sentence 'your work is dignified'?
options: Because she does not believe her work matters
Because saying it lets the speaker feel they have noticed something, after which nothing further is required
Because it is usually said sarcastically
Because it implies she should work harder
questions.explanation: 'the feeling of having noticed is, for them, the end of the matter ... the dignity, having been declared, requires nothing further.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 2 explains what the sentence accomplishes for the speaker.

questions.prompt: What 'less comfortable fact' does the writer say the dignity compliment helps the reader avoid?
options: That a person can do necessary work well and still find it hard, and still deserve more than a kind sentence
That the building is dirtier than it looks
That she resents the daytime workers
questions.explanation: 'a person can do necessary work, do it well, and still find it hard, and still deserve more than a kind sentence at a revolving door.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 3 states the uncomfortable fact directly.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say she would actually value, 'smaller and stranger than respect'?
options: A pay rise reflecting the difficulty of the work
Being known to exist in particular — as a named individual with a reason for working nights
A formal award from the company
Being moved to a daytime shift
questions.explanation: 'I would value being known to exist in particular. Not 'cleaners' as a noble abstraction, but the fact that the woman who empties your bin has a name.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer work at night?
options: The night rate is higher and she has a daughter's university fees to pay
She cannot work during daylight for health reasons
The daytime shifts were all taken
questions.explanation: 'the night rate is higher and she has a daughter at the University of Waterloo whose fees do not care how dignified anyone finds the labour that pays them.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by saying the reader has 'decided, without deciding' not to learn her name?
options: The reader consciously chose to ignore her
Through inattention rather than active choice, the reader has effectively chosen not to know her
The company forbids learning cleaners' names
The reader forgot her name after being told it
questions.explanation: 'you have decided, without deciding, that you will not.' — a default of non-attention that functions as a decision.
questions.hint: Paragraph 4; consider the paradox in the phrase.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'the off-hours architecture of the city'?
options: The buildings that are only open at night
The hidden network of night workers — cleaners, guards, bakers, nurses — who keep the city running while it sleeps
The architectural style of late-night venues
The lighting design of the city after dark
questions.explanation: 'the rhythm of the cleaners and guards and bakers and nurses who keep the place breathing while it sleeps.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 5 lists who makes up this 'architecture'.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop'?
options: The upper floors have no nameplates
Among the night workers everyone knows each other's names, but recognition does not extend up to the daytime workers
The daytime workers refuse to give their own names
The building's directory omits the upper floors
questions.explanation: 'We know each other's names down here. It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop.' — recognition flows among the night workers but not up the class divide.
questions.hint: Paragraph 5 contrasts 'down here' with 'upward, toward you'.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's final request to the reader?
options: To donate to her daughter's tuition fund
To ask whether calling work 'dignified' honours the worker or excuses the speaker from truly seeing one
To stop using the revolving door
To campaign for higher night wages
questions.explanation: 'ask yourself whether you are honouring the worker or excusing yourself from the harder labour of seeing one.'
questions.hint: The penultimate paragraph contains the single surviving request.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That meaning well is worthless and the polite readers are hypocrites
That her work is genuinely sometimes tedious, unpleasant, and lonely
That recognition exists among night workers but stops at the class divide
That a kind sentence can be a substitute for actually seeing a person
questions.explanation: She explicitly says 'you mean well and meaning well is not nothing' and 'Do not mistake this for bitterness.' She does not dismiss good intentions as worthless.
questions.hint: Look at how carefully she qualifies her criticism in paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: Which of these two paraphrases is closer to the writer's stance on the 'dignity of work'?
options: All work is dignified, and saying so is the kindest thing one can do
Declaring work 'dignified' can be a way of avoiding the harder act of recognising the individual who does it
Cleaning work is not dignified and should not be praised
Dignity is irrelevant; only higher wages matter
questions.explanation: 'insisting on it can be a way of avoiding the point' and the closing line about the sentence being 'so popular, and so cheap' — the slogan substitutes for real recognition.
questions.hint: She critiques the function of the slogan, not the worth of the work itself.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'a noble abstraction' (paragraph 4) is used to suggest that:
options: Cleaners are nobler than office workers
Praising 'cleaners' as a category lets one avoid seeing any actual cleaner as an individual
Abstract art is more dignified than realism
questions.explanation: 'Not 'cleaners' as a noble abstraction, but the fact that the woman who empties your bin has a name' — the abstraction erases the particular person.
questions.hint: She contrasts the abstraction with a named individual.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the kind sentence is 'so popular, and so cheap'?
options: Because it is short and easy to remember
Because honouring the worker and excusing oneself feel identical from the inside, so the cheap option masquerades as the generous one
Because employers encourage it to avoid raising wages
Because it is printed on company posters
questions.explanation: 'The two feel almost identical from the inside. That ... is exactly why the kind sentence is so popular, and so cheap.'
questions.hint: The final paragraph explains the link between the two feelings and the cheapness.

questions.prompt: The closing signature — 'the woman you will not see' — primarily functions to:
options: Reveal that the writer is in disguise
Reinforce the letter's central theme of invisibility and the failure of recognition
Suggest the writer is about to quit
Indicate the letter is anonymous for legal reasons
questions.explanation: The signature drives home the theme: despite sharing the building, she remains unseen — recognition stops 'upward'.
questions.hint: Connect the signature to the letter's argument about being known.

cloze.template:
To the people who arrive as I am leaving:

You have never seen me, though we share a building and, in a sense, a life. I clean the thirty-first floor between two and five in the morning, and by the time your lifts begin to fill at eight, I am asleep in a flat you would not be able to find. We overlap for perhaps four minutes a day, at the revolving door, where you are arriving {{b1}} and I am leaving emptied, and in those four minutes you are unfailingly polite and entirely {{b2}}. I am not writing to {{b3}} you for that. Incuriosity is not cruelty. I am writing because I have been thinking about a particular kind of kindness that I would rather you did not offer me.

Now and then one of you stops. You tell me, with real warmth, that what I do is important, that the building could not function without me, that cleaning is {{b4}} work and you have nothing but respect for it. I want to be careful here, because you mean well and meaning well is not nothing. But I have come to {{b5}} that sentence. It is a sentence people say to feel that they have noticed something, and the feeling of having noticed is, for them, the end of the matter. You say the work is dignified and then you go up to the thirty-first floor I have just made {{b6}} for you, and the dignity, having been declared, requires nothing further of either of us.

Here is the thing I would ask you to sit with, uncomfortably. The dignity of my work is not the point, and insisting on it can be a way of {{b7}} the point. A great deal of what I do is genuinely {{b8}}, and some of it is unpleasant, and on the bad nights it is lonely in a way that no amount of respect from a passing stranger can touch. To tell me it is dignified is to ask me to supply you with a {{b9}} story about myself, so that you do not have to hold the less comfortable fact that a person can do necessary work, do it well, and still find it hard.

What I would value is smaller and stranger than respect. I would value being known to exist in {{b10}}. Not 'cleaners' as a noble {{b11}}, but the fact that the woman who empties your bin has a name, which is on a card you have never read, and a reason she works at night, which is that the night rate is higher and she has a daughter at the University of Waterloo whose fees do not care how dignified anyone finds the labour that pays them. I am not asking you to learn this. I am pointing out that you could, and that you have decided, without deciding, that you will not.

Do not mistake this for {{b12}}. I am, on balance, alright. The work has given me a strange expertise in the off-hours architecture of the city — the rhythm of the cleaners and guards and bakers and nurses who keep the place breathing while it sleeps, a whole society that hands the building to yours at dawn like a relay {{b13}} you never notice you are taking. We are not invisible to each other. We know each other's names down here. It is only upward, toward you, that the names stop.

So I will leave you with the one request that survives all this. The next time someone tells you a kind of work is dignified, ask yourself whether you are {{b14}} the worker or excusing yourself from the harder labour of seeing one. The two feel almost identical from the inside. That, I have come to think, is exactly why the kind sentence is so popular, and so {{b15}}.

blanks.explanation: 'arriving caffeinated' — the precise, faintly ironic word for being wired on coffee, set against her being 'emptied'.
'unfailingly polite and entirely incurious' — incurious (lacking curiosity) is the exact pairing she goes on to discuss; 'polite' is already used in the same clause.
'I am not writing to scold you' — 'scold' (to tell off) is the warm, slightly maternal register the letter adopts.
'cleaning is dignified work' — 'dignified' is the exact word she quotes and then interrogates throughout the letter.
'I have come to distrust that sentence' — distrust (to regard with suspicion) fits 'have come to' and her measured tone better than blunt 'reject'.
'the thirty-first floor I have just made habitable for you' — 'habitable' (fit to occupy) elevates the cleaning into the moral point about who makes the space livable.
'a way of avoiding the point' — 'avoiding' matches the deliberate evasion she describes; 'missing' implies accident, which undercuts her argument.
'genuinely tedious' — 'tedious' (tiresomely long and dull) is the elevated register; the others are flatter near-synonyms.
'supply you with a comforting story' — 'comforting' precisely names the function: it soothes the listener, not the worker.
'being known to exist in particular' — 'in particular' (as a specific individual) is the fixed phrase that sets up the contrast with 'abstraction'.
'a noble abstraction' — 'abstraction' (a general idea detached from instances) is the precise antonym of 'in particular'.
'Do not mistake this for bitterness' — 'bitterness' (lingering resentment) is the exact feeling she disavows before saying she is 'on balance, alright'.
'like a relay baton you never notice you are taking' — a baton is what runners pass in a relay; the only word that completes the athletics metaphor.
'whether you are honouring the worker or excusing yourself' — 'honouring' best balances against 'excusing yourself' in her either/or test.
'so popular, and so cheap' — 'cheap' (costing nothing, morally hollow) carries the sting; the parallel 'so X, and so Y' demands the pointed word.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer argues that calling someone's work 'dignified' can be a way of excusing yourself from the harder labour of actually seeing the individual who does it. Think of a person whose work you rely on but whose name you have never learned. What would it change, for you and for them, to know them 'in particular' rather than as a category — and why is that recognition, as the writer suggests, so much harder than a kind word?
